_comment: D378 Cross Keys / Port Republic, June 8-9, 1862. Two adjacent Valley fields form one T8 scenario: Fremont attacks Ewell's ridge at Cross Keys, then Jackson attacks Tyler and Carroll at The Coaling. The roles and side-keyed home edges flip between phases. Army-present anchors, senior grades, outcomes, and landmarks follow the committed D377 contract; committed strengths, lower groupings, guns, coordinates, readiness, and timing remain Inferred or Unpinned. No scenario-specific combat or result control exists (D74).

sources: Encyclopedia Virginia - Shenandoah Valley Campaign of 1862
crossKeysPortRepublic.name: Cross Keys / Port Republic - The Valley Finale
crossKeysPortRepublic.place: Rockingham County, Virginia - Ewell's ridge south of Mill Creek, then Lewiston Lane and The Coaling above Port Republic
crossKeysPortRepublic.blurb: Fremont first attacks Ewell's high ground at Cross Keys with only part of his army committed. The next morning the axis reverses: Jackson concentrates against Tyler and Carroll at Port Republic, where Taylor's Louisiana Brigade must reach the wooded artillery knoll called The Coaling.
crossKeysPortRepublic.provenance: Verified for dates, army-present anchors, senior commander grades, outcomes, side-total casualty direction, and named terrain under the committed D377 source register. Inferred for committed strengths, lower groupings, gun envelopes, coordinates, readiness, and timing. The Coaling battery identity remains Unpinned.
crossKeysPortRepublic._fogNote: Both phases declare defaultFog:false. The packet's early-morning haze at Port Republic belongs to presentation; the woods-covered approach carries the flank geometry without tactical fog.
crossKeysPortRepublic._homeEdgeNote: The top-level map matches Cross Keys. Each phase declares its own side-keyed home edges because the field and attack axis reverse at Port Republic. All coordinate orientations are Inferred engine abstractions.

weather.note: The presentation uses a light morning haze for the June 8-9 sequence. Tactical fog stays off in both phases; the wooded route to The Coaling is terrain, not a visibility bonus.

menu.title: CROSS KEYS / PORT REPUBLIC (1862)
menu.deck: Two fields in two days: test Fremont's limited commitment against Ewell's ridge, then reverse the armies and fight for The Coaling at Port Republic.
menu.aria: Cross Keys and Port Republic, June 1862 - a two-phase real-time historical battle with the attacking and defending armies reversing between fields; command either army.

sides.heading: Cross Keys / Port Republic - Take Which Command?
sides.intro: June 8-9, 1862 - Ewell must hold the Valley escape route at Cross Keys; Jackson then concentrates against Tyler and Carroll at Port Republic.

US.title: Command the United States (Fremont / Tyler)
US.deck: At Cross Keys, cross Mill Creek and dislodge Ewell from the ridge without mistaking Fremont's army present for men committed. At Port Republic, hold Lewiston Lane and keep the guns on The Coaling.

CS.title: Command the Confederacy (Ewell / Jackson)
CS.deck: Use Ewell's high ground to stop Fremont on June 8. On June 9, reverse the axis and concentrate Jackson's force against The Coaling before the Union line can stabilize.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. Cross Keys carries weight 1; Port Republic carries the decisive weight 3. The game models the two fights, not Jackson's campaign-wide interior lines.

brief.attack: Fremont has more men in the Valley than he puts onto this field. Cross Mill Creek with the committed grouping and attack Ewell's ridge under cautious doctrine. Port Republic reverses your role: Tyler and Carroll defend Lewiston Lane and the generic Union artillery emplacement at The Coaling.
brief.defend: Hold Ewell's ridge south of Mill Creek and keep the Port Republic Road open. The next phase reverses your role and your home edge: Jackson attacks from Port Republic, with Taylor's brigade using the wooded approach toward The Coaling.

objective.name: Ewell's Ridge south of Mill Creek

phases.timing: June 8, 1862 - the Union advance south of Mill Creek
phases._homeEdgeNote: Increasing z points from Fremont's approach across Mill Creek toward Ewell's ridge and Port Republic. This is an Inferred engine orientation, not a Verified cardinal map.

objective.name: Ewell's Ridge south of Mill Creek

transition.title: Cross Keys - the road must stay open
transition.lead: Fremont advances across Mill Creek toward Ewell's high ground. The modeled United States force is an Inferred committed grouping below the 11,500-man army-present anchor; men not committed do not receive a hidden penalty because they are not fielded.

hills.name: Ewell's Ridge south of Mill Creek

hills.name: Trimble's ridge on the Confederate right
hills.note: Trimble's ridge anchors the right-sector terrain. The modeled grouping on it remains Inferred. (Verified terrain name; Inferred placement)

woods.note: Wooded ground breaks the approach to the Confederate right. (Verified landscape class; Inferred placement)
Farm woodlots screen the left of Ewell's high ground. (Inferred map abstraction)

swamps.note: The Mill Creek bottom slows Fremont's advance without causing damage. (Verified obstacle; Inferred footprint)

markers.name: Port Republic Road

markers.note: A navigation landmark at the crossroads, not the scored objective. (Verified feature; Inferred placement)

reinforcements.name: Mountain Department Advance - Reserve Grouping

teaching.head: Army present is not army committed
teaching.body: The 11,500-man figure describes Fremont's force present. The game fields an Inferred 7,900-man commitment and labels every lower grouping accordingly. Cautious doctrine, Mill Creek, and the ridge carry the result; no hidden hesitation or firepower control exists.
teaching.provenance: Verified army-present anchor; Inferred commitment and all simulation detail.

phases.name: Port Republic - The Coaling
phases.sector: The South Fork, Lewiston Lane, the wheat field, and the wooded artillery knoll at The Coaling
phases.timing: June 9, 1862 - the morning attack east of Port Republic
phases._homeEdgeNote: Increasing z points from Port Republic toward Lewiston Lane. This reverses the Cross Keys side-keyed home edges and remains an Inferred engine orientation.

transition.title: Port Republic - the axis reverses
transition.lead: Ewell has held Fremont away from the concentration route. On the next field Jackson attacks Tyler and Carroll along Lewiston Lane. Taylor's Louisiana Brigade approaches The Coaling through woods; the battery identity on the knoll remains Unpinned.

woods.note: Taylor's Louisiana Brigade used the woods-covered route toward The Coaling. (Verified route class; Inferred footprint)
Wooded ground wraps the artillery knoll and breaks the direct line of approach. (Verified terrain class; Inferred footprint)
Farm woods border the Union right near Lewiston Lane. (Inferred map abstraction)

CS.note: Winder commanded the Stonewall Brigade at Port Republic. Grade and formation identity are Verified; engine quality and coordinates are Inferred.

CS.note: The Stonewall Brigade identity and Winder's command are source-anchored; strength and exact placement remain modeled. Verified identity; Inferred strength.

reinforcements.name: Jackson's Supporting Grouping
reinforcements.entry: concentrating from Port Republic behind the opening brigades
reinforcements.note: The support element is required only by the simulation grain and carries no invented formation identity. Inferred grouping; Inferred committed strength.

teaching.head: A wooded route, not a flank bonus
teaching.body: Taylor's brigade reaches The Coaling through modeled woods while Jackson's full phase force stays inside the packet's 6,000-man anchor. The artillery position is Verified, but its battery identity and all lower splits remain Unpinned or Inferred.
teaching.provenance: Verified formation and terrain anchors; Inferred strength, guns, placement, and timing.

US.US: History diverges across both fields. Fremont forces Ewell from the Cross Keys ridge, and the Union line then keeps The Coaling. Jackson cannot concentrate safely against Shields's detached command.
US.CS: Cross Keys diverges, Port Republic holds to history. Fremont breaks Ewell's ridge, but Jackson still takes The Coaling on the decisive second field. The weighted result favors the Port Republic concentration.

CS.US: Cross Keys holds to history, Port Republic diverges. Ewell keeps the road open, but Tyler and Carroll deny The Coaling and stop Jackson's concentration on June 9.
CS.CS: History holds. Ewell checks Fremont at Cross Keys; Jackson then concentrates against Tyler and Carroll and takes The Coaling at Port Republic. The battlefield result comes from fielded strength, ground, doctrine, and timing.

endNote.draw: The two fields divide without a decisive weighted result. The historical sequence ended with Confederate victories at Cross Keys and Port Republic, preserving Jackson's Valley concentration.

teaching.title: Cross Keys / Port Republic - what the two fields can teach

cards.head: Two days, two fields
cards.body: Ewell's June 8 defense at Cross Keys kept Fremont away from the concentration route. Jackson attacked Tyler and Carroll at Port Republic on June 9. The game joins those adjacent fights while keeping their fields and command roles distinct.

cards.head: Eleven thousand five hundred present, fewer committed
cards.body: The 11,500-man figure is Fremont's army present at Cross Keys, not a pinned committed subtotal. This scenario fields 7,900 in disclosed Inferred groupings. Men outside the modeled commitment receive no hidden penalty because they are absent from the tactical OOB.

cards.head: The Coaling - emplacement known, battery Unpinned
cards.body: The wooded knoll on the Union left was the decisive artillery position at Port Republic, and Taylor's Louisiana Brigade used the wooded approach against it. The packet does not pin the battery or the exact storming regiments, so the game names neither.

cards.head: Three armies, one valley
cards.body: Banks, Fremont, and Shields never combined against Jackson. Massanutten screened movement between separated columns. That operational problem belongs in teaching: the tactical engine scores only the two fields placed before the player.

cards.head: Foot cavalry, without sainthood
cards.body: The campaign depended on forced marching, secrecy, interior lines, exhaustion, and straggling. The packet does not pin a scholarly mileage total, so this card stays count-free and gives Jackson no speed, morale, or genius control. Lost Cause deification is not a simulation input.

cards.head: Kernstown - a tactical loss with strategic effect
cards.body: Jackson lost at Kernstown after underestimating the Union force. Washington's response helped his later campaign, but the battlefield result remains a Union victory. Kernstown stays teaching-only here; the game does not rewrite the repulse into a Jackson win.

cards.head: McDowell - victory did not mean fewer losses
cards.body: The Confederates held the field at McDowell while suffering the heavier loss total. That fight remains teaching-only and guards against importing a winner-bleeds-less assumption into other battles.

codex.title: Cross Keys and Port Republic, June 8-9, 1862
codex.summary: Ewell held the high ground south of Mill Creek at Cross Keys while Fremont committed only part of the force present. The next morning Jackson concentrated at Port Republic against Tyler and Carroll, where Taylor's Louisiana Brigade reached The Coaling through the woods. Both fields ended in Confederate victories. The campaign's defining interior lines and feints remain outside this tactical objective-hold engine.

axes.scope: Two tactical fields; the campaign's operational maneuver is not simulated
